etymology: Middle English, from Old French grace, from Latin gratia 'pleasing quality, favor, gratitude'

senses.definition: Simple elegance or refinement of movement.
senses.bible_definition: The free and unmerited favor of God, as manifested in the salvation of sinners and the bestowal of blessings.

examples.text: For by grace are ye saved through faith.

senses.definition: Courteous goodwill; a disposition to be generous or helpful.

etymology: Late Middle English, from Latin creat- 'produced', from the verb creare

senses.bible_definition: Used of God's act of bringing the universe and all living things into being (ex nihilo).

examples.text: In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth.

etymology: Old English hlāford 'master of a household', from hlāf 'loaf' + weard 'keeper'

senses.definition: Someone or something having power, authority, or influence; a master or ruler.
senses.bible_definition: In the KJV, LORD (small capitals) represents the divine name Yahweh; Lord refers to Adonai or the New Testament Kyrios.

examples.text: The LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want.

senses.definition: A title given to a peer (a man of high rank in the British aristocracy).

etymology: Middle English, from Old French, present participle of covenir 'agree', from Latin convenire

senses.definition: An agreement, usually formal and solemn.
senses.bible_definition: A solemn, binding agreement between God and his people, often sealed by a visible sign (rainbow, circumcision, etc.).

examples.text: And I will establish my covenant between me and thee.

senses.definition: The quality of being morally right or justifiable.
senses.bible_definition: Right standing before God; the moral quality of conforming to God's law; also the imputed righteousness received through faith in Christ.

examples.text: But seek ye first the kingdom of God, and his righteousness.

etymology: Middle English feith, from Old French feid, from Latin fides 'trust, faith'

senses.definition: Complete trust or confidence in someone or something.
senses.bible_definition: The assurance of things hoped for and the conviction of things not seen; trust and belief in God and his promises.

examples.text: Now faith is the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen.
For we walk by faith, not by sight.

etymology: Old English behealdan 'to hold, observe', from be- + healdan 'hold'

senses.definition: See or observe (someone or something, especially of a remarkable or impressive nature).
senses.bible_definition: Used as an exclamation to draw attention to something important or surprising (KJV equivalent of 'look!' or 'see!').

examples.text: Behold, I stand at the door, and knock.

etymology: Middle English, from Old French salvacion, from Latin salvatio(n-), from salvare 'to save'

senses.definition: Preservation or deliverance from harm, ruin, or loss.
senses.bible_definition: Deliverance from sin and its consequences; eternal life granted through faith in Jesus Christ.

examples.text: For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son.

etymology: Late Middle English, from Old French sanctifier, from Latin sanctificare, from sanctus 'holy'

senses.definition: Set apart as or declare holy; consecrate.
senses.bible_definition: To make holy or set apart for God's purposes; the progressive work of the Holy Spirit in conforming a believer to the image of Christ.

examples.text: And God blessed the seventh day, and sanctified it.

etymology: Late Middle English, from Old French redimer or Latin redimere 'buy back'

senses.definition: Compensate for the faults or bad aspects of (something); buy the freedom of.
senses.bible_definition: To buy back or ransom; Christ's act of paying the penalty for sin to free humanity from its bondage.

examples.text: Christ hath redeemed us from the curse of the law.
